1:1.text: Israel. The dignity of the author, and the importance of the subject, invite us to read.
Solomon is the first whose name is placed at the head of any work in Scripture.

1:2.text: To know. This is the design of these parables.

They tend to instruct both the unexperienced and the wise, ver. 5. There are three sorts of wisdom: the divine, which is God himself; (chap. 3:16.) the supernatural, which is his gift, to lead us into all virtue; and the worldly, which is mixed with error, etc.

1:5.text: Wiser. "Tamdiu audiendum et discendum est, quamdiu nescias, et si proverbio credimus, quamdiu vivas," says Seneca, ep. 77.
Governments. And be fit to govern others, (Worthington) as well as himself.

1:6.text: Sayings. This science was much esteemed, 3 Kings 10:1., and Ecclesiasticus 39:2.

1:7.text: Fear. Thus we arrive at charity. (St. Augustine, in ep. Jo. ix.; Job 28:28., etc.) This fear includes religion, but not barren speculations.
It implies a desire to act, and not simply to understand.

1:8.text: Mother. The first precept is to learn of our elders, and the second to resist evil counsels, ver. 10.
Our parents have the greatest influence over us. Solomon presupposes that they are virtuous and well informed.

1:12.text: Pit. Grave, or hell, like Dathan, Numbers xvi. This shews the greatest rage, Job 31:31.

1:17.text: Wings. If thou attend, therefore, to my instructions, their arts will be vain.
They unjustly seek to deceive the pious.
Watchfulness will be the best protection against them.

1:19.text: While they attempt to invade another's property, they ruin themselves, and come to the gallows.

1:20.text: Streets. In every place we may learn wisdom. "The wise learn more from fools, than fools do from the wise," as Cato well observed.

1:22.text: Fools. Hebrew, "and scorners delight in their scorning."
Such are the pests of society.
They turn piety to ridicule, and will talk about things which they do not understand, like our esprits forts, (Calmet) or pretended philosophers.

1:26.text: Mock. God is too much above us to act thus; but he will treat us as an enraged enemy.
In hell, the damned will cry in vain, ver. 28. They had sufficient graces offered while they were alive.

1:28.text: Find me. Because their repentance was false, like that of Antiochus, 2 Machabees 9:13., and Psalm 11:4.

1:30.text: Despised. Literally, "detracted," (Haydock) supposing my threats would not be put in execution. Hebrew, "they abhorred."

1:32.text: Turning. Hebrew, "the ease of the simple," who have given way to deceit.
Them. The objects of their eager desires, prove their ruin, Ezechiel 16:49.

1:33.text: Evils. Both the just and the wicked, (ver. 31.; Haydock) shall be treated according to their deserts, 2 Corinthians 5:10.
Even in this world, the just enjoy the peace of a good conscience.

2:1.text: If. This proves free will. To become truly wise, we must desire it with the same avidity as a miser seeks for riches.
We must also pray, (ver. 3.) with humility (ver. 2.) to God, the giver of wisdom, ver. 6. Every science which has not Him for the beginning and end, is vain and dangerous.

2:7.text: Salvation. Protestants, "sound wisdom." Tushiya (Haydock) often occurs in this book, and is very comprehensive, (Calmet) denoting the substance (Haydock) of goodness, virtue, etc., Job 5:12.

2:8.text: God gives them grace to follow virtue, (Haydock) and protects them from every danger.

2:12.text: Things. The danger of such company for young people is very great, as the heart is misled by the understanding, (Calmet) if it do not itself shew the way to error.
We all possess a fund of corruption.

2:14.text: Evil. These signs prove whom we ought to shun. St. Augustine depicts his own conduct before his conversion in the darkest colours.

2:16.text: Strange. Idolatress, (Grotius) or rather the abandoned woman, whether married or not.

This description, as well as the former, guards us against heretics, who leave the right way of the Catholic Church, (ver. 13.) and devise perverse doctrines of rebellion against princes, etc., as if faith alone were sufficient. By this faith, Protestants do not mean any article which all must believe, but only that each one must be convinced that he himself is just, and will be saved. Like the strange woman, they preach a comfortable doctrine, and use sweet speeches, Romans 16:18. Their conversion is a very difficult matter, (ver. 19.) because they are condemned by their own judgment, (Titus 3:11.) and will not admit of the ordinary means of instruction in the Church.

2:17.text: Youth. Her husband, whom she married young when love is more lasting. This increases her guilt. It may also signify her father, or preceptor.

2:18.text: God. The law forbids adultery, and all impurities, Deuteronomy 23:17., and Leviticus 20:10. Both the parties, guilty of adultery, were punished with death.

2:19.text: Life. A sincere conversion is so rare among people once addicted to impurity, (Haydock) which resembles a deep pit.

2:21.text: Earth. Happily. This was the wish of the carnal Jews. But the more enlightened raise their thoughts to heaven.

3:1.text: My son. God speaks, or the master instructs his disciple, ver. 21.
We must remember and love instruction, and reduce it to practice.

3:2.text: Peace. These prefigured more substantial blessings.

3:3.text: Truth. Be kind and faithful to all, Genesis 24:27., and Psalm 24:10.

3:4.text: Good. Septuagint, "forecast good before the Lord and men." St. Paul seems to allude to this version, Romans 12:17., and 2 Corinthians 8:22.

3:5.text: All. God will have nothing by halves.
Lean not. By pride, 2 Corinthians 12:13. All must be referred to God.
In him we may safely trust.

3:8.text: Bones. Thou shalt enjoy perfect health.

3:9.text: Fruits. Septuagint add, "of justice;" to intimate that no presents will be acceptable, which have been unjustly acquired.

3:11.text: Him. Correction is rather a proof of love than of anger, Psalm 3:19.
God thus shews that he approves of his servants; and therefore his other promises, which seem of a temporal nature, must be understood with reference to the next life.

3:12.text: And as. Septuagint, "but he scourgeth every son whom he receiveth," as St. Paul quotes this passage, Hebrews 12:6. The verb is now wanting in Hebrew, or ceab; "as a father" may signify "scourgeth, in piel, (Menochius) with i prefixed.

3:16.text: Glory. Her hands are full, and she grants favours with profusion, (Haydock) as with both hands, promising eternal life.

3:18.text: Life. Like that planted in paradise, Genesis 2:9.

3:19.text: Wisdom. Hitherto he had spoken of that virtue. Now he treats of the uncreated wisdom, (Calmet) which is God the Son.

3:20.text: Out. The higher and lower waters being divided, (Calmet) or the channels formed to receive the waters.
It may also speak of the deluge.
With. Hebrew, "have distilled dew." This is so copious, as to resemble rain in Palestine, Judges 6:37., and 2 Kings 17:12.

3:22.text: Mouth. There have been wise and virtuous men afflicted, ver. 11. But they have borne all with patience, and have merited a more glorious reward than what this earth could afford.

3:27.text: Able. Protestants, "withhold not good from them to whom it is due, when it is in the power of thine hand to do it." Relieve the distressed.
They have a title to that wealth, since those who possess it are bound to relieve the indigent.
Septuagint, "refrain not from doing good," etc.

3:28.text: Present. Alms in season are doubly valuable.
Antigonus acquired the title of Doson, "about to give," as he never gave, (Plutarch) but only promised.

3:30.text: Cause. We may defend ourselves; but herein great discretion is necessary.
Cum pari contendere anceps est: cum superiore furiosum; cum inferiore sordidum.

3:31.text: Ways. Of injustice. Seek not to attain his prosperity by the same means.

3:34.text: Scorners. Literally, "he will delude the scorners."
He will treat them as they would treat others, Psalm 17:27.
Septuagint, "the Lord resisteth the proud," etc. So the apostles quote this passage, 1 Peter 5:5., and James 4:6.

3:35.text: Disgrace. They are seen by more, and their fall is more dangerous.

4:1.text: Father. He shews the greatest respect for his parents, ver. 3.

4:3.text: But Solomon was not the only son of Bethsabee, as St. Luke 3:23. specifies Nathan, his brother. See 2 Kings 12:24. Septuagint have "beloved in," etc.

4:4.text: As David instructed his son, so the latter teaches all how they may learn wisdom.

4:7.text: Beginning. The first part of wisdom is to desire it. For nothing hindereth from being just, but that justice is not desired.
"Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get," etc.
It is the one thing necessary.
The pearl, to acquire which we must part with every thing else, if required, Matthew 13:46.

4:17.text: Wickedness. Acquired thereby, or they make sin their food, Proverbs 26:6.

4:18.text: Day. They are children of light, (John 12:35.) and give edification, always advancing in virtue.

4:19.text: Fall. They are unconcerned about sin, and neither avoid it, nor strive to repent.

4:22.text: Flesh. All may derive benefit from their consideration.

4:23.text: From it. As the heart is the principal part of the body, so the will is the chief power of the soul, from which good or evil proceeds.
A clean heart gives life, a corrupt one, death, Matthew 15:11, 19.

4:24.text: From thee. Neither detract, nor give any countenance to detractors.

4:25.text: Steps. Be attentive to thy own affairs, Proverbs 17:24.

4:26.text: Straight. Hebrew, "ponder." Examine what thou takest in hand, and walk not at random, Hebrews 12:13.

4:27.text: For, etc. What follows in not in Hebrew or the Complutensian (Calmet) Septuagint. But it is in the Roman [Septuagint], etc., (Haydock) and in the new edition of St. Jerome, as it was explained by Ven. Bede. Lyranus and Cajetan reject it.

5:2.text: Thoughts. Or wisdom; and act with discretion.
Mind, etc., is omitted in Hebrew and St. Jerome.
By woman all concupiscence, or the inducement to sin, is commonly understood. We must not think of such things.

5:4.text: Sword. "It is a crime even to hearken." (St. Ambrose, de Abrah. 2:11.) She seeks thy ruin, ver. 5., and Proverbs 2:16.

5:6.text: They. Hebrew, "if perhaps thou ponder the path of life."
Or "she ponders not," etc. She walks inconsiderately, and consults only her passions, Proverbs 7:10.
No one can depend on her love.

5:9.text: Strangers. The world, the flesh, and the devil are such; cruelly devising our ruin.

5:10.text: Strength. Or children, ver. 16., and Genesis 49:3.

5:11.text: Body. He alludes to a shameful disease, the just punishment of intemperance, Ecclesiasticus 19:3.

5:14.text: Evil. Infirm and worn out, having lost my reputation, etc.
Though I lived among the faithful, I was under no restraint.

5:15.text: Well. Live comfortably on your own property, (Cajetan) with your own wife.

5:16.text: Waters. Mayst thou have a numerous offspring, (ver. 10.) and be liberal. Many copies of the Septuagint, etc., have a negation, with Aquila, "let not thy," etc., (Calmet) though it may be read with an interrogation, "are the waters of thy fountain to be?" etc.
By no means. Origen (in Numbers xii.) acknowledges both readings.
Good instructions must be given to those who are well disposed, but not to scoffers, or obstinate infidels.

Husbands are exhorted to be content with their own wives, (ver. 15, 20.) so that the negative particle seems to be here wanting, as it is, Proverbs 6:17., in Manuscript 60, (Kennicott) and Proverbs 14:33.

5:17.text: Thee. Stick to thy own wife. In a moral sense, let those who instruct others, take care not to neglect themselves.

5:18.text: Vein. Thou shalt have a numerous progeny, Psalm 67:28., and Isaias 48:1.

5:19.text: Love. This is spoken by way of permission, and to withdraw people from unlawful connections, Ecclesiastes 2:1., and 1 Corinthians 7:29.

5:22.text: Ropes. "Evil habits unrestrained induce a necessity," (St. Augustine, Confessions 8:5.) though not absolute.
The libertine thinks he can get free as soon as he pleases; not being aware of the chains which he is forging for himself.

6:1.text: Hand. Agreements were made by shaking hands, Isaias 62:8.
Stranger. Septuagint, "enemy." He will presently be such, or thy friend's creditor will soon lay hold on thee. By standing surety for another, we expose ourselves to be ruined by his negligence.
The Persians had a horror chiefly of lying and debts.
All sureties are not condemned, but only such as are inconsiderate.
A diligent compliance with engagements is commended.

6:3.text: Make. Hebrew, "humble thyself, and made sure thy friend," (Protestant) entreating (Haydock) and forcing him to pay his debts. The Fathers apply this to pastors, who have undertaken to direct others. Their soul is at stake.

6:8.text: Harvest. The economy and diligence of this little republic is admirable.

Some copies of the Septuagint add with St. Ambrose, (Hex. V. []1., etc.; Calmet) "or go to the bee, and behold what a worker it is, and how beautiful is its work; whose labours kings and private people use for health. But it is desirable and glorious to all; and though it be weak in strength, by the love of wisdom it has got forward" (Haydock) in esteem.
Nature has given the form of a monarchy in bees, and of a democracy in the regulations of the ant.

6:11.text: A traveller. Septuagint add, "wicked," and Hebrew gives the idea of a robber.
But, etc. This is not in Hebrew, Complutensian, or St. Jerome.

6:12.text: Apostate. Hebrew, "of Belial,["] without restraint of religion and law.
Every one who sins through malice and particularly heretics, employ all their members to pervert others.
Mouth. No reliance can be had on his promises.

6:13.text: Finger. These signs imply haughtiness, etc., Psalm 34:19., and Isaias 58:9. The posture indicates the interior sentiments, (St. Ambrose, off. 1:18.) insomuch, that St. Ambrose would not receive among the clergy one whose gestures were too light. The Persians still speak by signs.

6:16.text: Detesteth. This expression does not always mean that the last is worse than the former.
All the six sins are damnable, but the seventh is here most so, being against charity and unity, and the devil's sin.
Lying seems to be reprobated by three different terms.

6:23.text: Instruction. Given for our improvement, (Haydock) with charity. See Deuteronomy 6:6., and Psalm 18:9.

6:24.text: Stranger. This is often inculcated, because nothing is more dangerous in youth, nor more contrary to the study of wisdom.

6:26.text: Woman. Who is married, exposes her lover to the danger of death. She chooses the most accomplished men, while the harlot receives the first comer.

6:27.text: Burn. No one can deal with an adulteress without guilt.
All probable occasions of sin must be shunned.

6:29.text: Clean. Or be left unpunished. No crime disturbs the order of society so much, nor is pardoned with more difficulty.

6:30.text: The fault is not so great, etc. The sin of theft is not so great, as to be compared with adultery: especially when a person pressed with hunger (which is the case here spoken of) steals to satisfy nature. Moreover the damage done by theft may much more easily be repaired, than the wrong done by adultery. But this does not hinder but that theft also is a mortal sin, forbidden by one of the ten commandments.
Hebrew, "they will not despise a thief, when he hath stolen to fill his soul, when he is hunger."

This was commonly supposed to be his motive, and he was only condemned to make restitution, without any further disgrace, Proverbs 19:24., and Exodus 22:1. But what necessity could the adulterer plead? Both he and the woman must suffer death, Leviticus 20:10.

6:31.text: Seven-fold. Or as much as may be required. The law never subjected the thief to restore above five-fold. If he had not enough, his person might be sold.

6:32.text: Folly. Literally, "want," inopiam. Hebrew, "is faint-hearted, corrupting his own soul, he will do that."

6:35.text: Gifts. "A husband would rather hear that his wife had been slain, than that she had been defiled."

7:1.text: Words. As dangerous temptations always threaten, the same instructions are frequently inculcated.

7:5.text: Thine. But another's, ver. 19. Give thy heart to wisdom, that it may be guarded against impure love.

7:6.text: Lattice. No glass was used, on account of the great heat.

7:7.text: Ones. Not in age, but prudence.

7:10.text: Attire. Covered with a veil, (Genesis 38:15.) though different from that of honest women.
The wanton are commonly the most gaudy; nullarum fere pretiosior cultus est quam quarum pudor vilis est.
Prepared, etc. Hebrew, "guarded," (Calmet) or "subtle of heart." (Protestants) "who makes the hearts of youths take flight."

7:11.text: "She is loud and stubborn, her feet abide not in her house."
Chaste women are guarded in their speech, and keep at home.

7:14.text: Prosperity. Or thy welfare, (Cornelius a Lapide; Tirinus) so great is my love for thee.
Vows. And therefore I have a feast prepared. People might carry home the greatest part of the victim to eat, if they were clean, Leviticus 7:29.

7:16.text: Cords. For greater ease, instead of boards, (Menochius) or the curtains are hung with precious cords from Egypt.

7:17.text: Aloes. Of Syria, (John 19:39.) different from ours, Numbers 34:6.

7:18.text: Inebriated. Protestants, "take our fill of love until the morning: let us solace ourselves with loves."
This passion is a sort of intoxication.

7:19.text: She speaks thus out of contempt.

7:20.text: The day. Septuagint, "after many days." Hebrew, "at the day concealed," or when the people will dwell under tents, (Chaldean; Calmet) or "at the new moon," when it does not appear.
He will not return for a long time, so that we need apprehend no danger from him.

7:22.text: Lamb. Protestants, "fool to the correction of the stocks," (Haydock) or "like a shackle (abs.) for the chastisement of a fool."
Sinners who have given way to temptations, are as inconsiderate as oxen, or birds which hasten to their own ruin.

7:26.text: Her. Solomon gave a melancholy proof of this, as well as David, and Amnon.

7:27.text: Death. There can be no precaution too great, Proverbs 2:18.

8:1.text: Voice. Men are wanting to themselves: they cannot plead ignorance.
Wisdom stands on high in the Catholic Church inviting all to virtue and happiness.
Some explain this of the light which is communicated to men; but the Fathers apply it to Jesus Christ, some of the expressions regarding his divine, and others his human nature, Ecclesiasticus xxiv.

8:3.text: Doors. Amid disputants, whose eagerness ought to convince us of the preference due to wisdom over all terrestrial concerns, ver. 10.

8:12.text: Thoughts. All good comes from God, the eternal wisdom, (Calmet) which speaks here.

8:15.text: Things. Power and knowledge are the gift of the Almighty, Romans 12:1. A prince who resembles God the most, is his best present.

8:18.text: But here it only means great, Isaias ix 15., and 61:6. Riches too commonly nourish pride, and it is very rare to see them joined with justice.

8:19.text: Stone. So the Septuagint translate paz, (Haydock) which designates a more pure sort of gold, Genesis 2:11.

8:21.text: Enrich. Hebrew, "grant what is (real goods) an inheritance to them," etc.
Treasures. Septuagint add, "with goods. If I announce to you daily occurrences, I will admonish you to number the things of the world," (Haydock) and all past events.

8:22.text: Possessed. As Christ was with God, equal to him in eternity, John 1:Septuagint, "created," which many of the Fathers explain of the word incarnate, (see Cornelius a Lapide; Bossuet) or he hath "placed me," (St. Athanasius 3:contra Arian. Eusebius) a pattern of all virtues. The Septuagint generally render kana, "possessed," as Aquila does here.

8:23.text: Up. Hebrew, "anointed." Septuagint, "he founded." Christ was appointed to be the foundation, on which we must be built.

8:24.text: Conceived. Having yet manifested none of my works. Since the creation, wisdom only seeks to communicate itself to us.

8:26.text: Poles. Hebrew, "head or height of the dust of the world."
I subsisted with the chaos, before things appeared in their present form.
The poles denote the north and south, or the four quarters of the world.

8:28.text: Sky. Protestants, "clouds." Pagnin, "the air." Vulgate aethera. Septuagint, "the clouds above."
Moses assigns the higher and lower waters the same origin, Genesis 1:7.

8:29.text: Pass. This is often remarked, Psalm 41:8.

8:30.text: Forming. Hebrew, "one nursed," (Calmet) or nursing, nutritius.
He was not an idle spectator.
Playing. With ease and surprising variety.

8:31.text: Men. God saw that all was good, but delighted most in his own image.
He prefers man before all other corporeal creatures.
To him alone below he has granted understanding, and a soul capable of virtue. The Son has also assumed our nature, Baruch 3:37.

8:35.text: Lord. Wisdom, or Jesus Christ, is our salvation, happiness, and life. Septuagint, "and the will is prepared by the Lord." St. Augustine often quotes this to prove the necessity of preventing grace.

8:36.text: Death. Not in itself, (Haydock) but by adhering to such things as bring death.

9:1.text: House. The sacred humanity, (St. Ignatius; St. Augustine, City of God 17:20.) or the Church.
Here we may receive all instruction, the seven sacraments, and the gifts of the Holy Ghost. Pleasure had mentioned her attractions: now those of true wisdom are set before us.

God sent his pastors at all times, to invite people to embrace the latter. They are all included in the number seven, both before and under the law, as well as in the gospel, where St. Paul styles Sts. Peter, James, and John, pillars, Galatians 2:This is the literal sense, on which the mystical is grounded, and both are intended by the Holy Ghost, intimating that the uncreated wisdom took flesh of the blessed Virgin Mary, prepared the table of bread and wine, as Priest according to the order of Melchisedec, and chose the weak of this world to confound the strong, as St. Augustine explains this passage.

9:2.text: Victims. Moses ordered the blood to be poured out at the door of the tabernacle, and a part given to the priests, after which the rest might be taken away. The like was probably done at Jerusalem, Leviticus 17:4. These victims are contrasted with those of pleasure, Proverbs 7:14.

Mingled. It was not customary for any but barbarians and the gods to take pure wine. Some mixed two, others three, five, or even twenty parts of water. But the scholiast of Aristophanes says, the best method was to have three parts of water, and two of wine. Mercury complains that his wine was half water.
The Fathers often apply this text to the feast of Jesus Christ in the blessed Eucharist.
St. Cyprian (ep. iii.) citeth the whole passage of Christ's sacrifice in the forms of bread and wine.

9:3.text: Maids. Septuagint, "servant men," the pastors of the church, inviting all to piety in so public a manner, that none can plead ignorance.
To invite. Protestants, "she crieth upon the highest places of the city."
Christ enjoins his apostles to preach on the roofs, Matthew 10:37.

9:4.text: One. Simple, but not inconstant, like children, 1 Corinthians 14:20. Pleasure addresses the same, (chap. 7:7.) but for their destruction.

9:7.text: Scorner. This is the reason why wisdom speaks only to the simple. The conceited would only laugh at her instructions. These scoffers represent heretics and libertines, Proverbs 1:22.
Where there is no hope of amendment, prudence and charity require us to be silent, as our rebukes would only procure us enmity, and make the sinner worse.
Of such St. John was afraid, and therefore ceased from writing, 3 John 9. Yet St. Paul commands public reprehension, 1 Timothy 5:20.

9:7.src: St. Augustine, City of God 1:9.; and St. Basil, reg. fus. 158.; Worthington
9:7.text: When there is any prospect of good, all, particularly superiors, are bound to correct.

9:9.text: Occasion. This word is found in Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic. We might supply instruction, (Calmet) with Protestants.

9:10.text: Prudence. Or "prudence is the science of the saints," (Haydock) directing what to choose on all occasions to obtain heaven.
The knowledge contained in the holy Scriptures, and possessed by the saints, is superior to all other sciences.

9:13.text: And full. Protestants, "she is simple and knoweth nothing." Septuagint, "is in want of a piece of bread." They have several verses before this, which are here omitted.
Wisdom and pleasure are opposed to each other.

9:17.text: Pleasant. Impure pleasures are more delightful (Calmet) to sensual men.

9:18.text: Giants. Who lived when all flesh had corrupted its ways, (Genesis 6:12.) and were sentenced to hell, Job 26:5., and Isaias 14:9.

10:1.text: Mother. A virtuous child cannot be indifferent to the joy of his parents.

10:2.text: Wickedness. Riches ill acquired, or tending to corrupt the heart, Luke 16:9.

10:3.text: Famine. Psalm 36:25. The prophets and Lazarus rejoice in suffering.

10:4.text: Poverty. Even of those who had plenty. This is true in a spiritual sense likewise.
The kingdom of heaven suffereth violence.
He, etc. This is not in Hebrew, Greek, St. Jerome, or in several Latin copies.
We find it in the Septuagint, Proverbs 9:13.
Away. He derives no benefits from lies.

10:5.text: He. Septuagint, "a son well educated shall be wise, and shall have the unwise for his servant. An intelligent son has been saved from the heat. But the wicked son is destroyed by the wind in time of harvest."
A good part of this is not in the original; yet it is received by the Greeks.

10:6.text: Wicked. Or, as the Hebrew seems to indicate, "the wicked covereth iniquity, by an hypocritical exterior," (Calmet) or, "the injury" (Mont.[Montanus?]) done to another, (chamas.; Haydock) "unseasonable, or infinite mourning," penthos auron.

10:7.text: Rot. Hebrew, "stink." His reputation shall be lost, Genesis 34:30.

10:8.text: Lips. He will not bear correction.
But suffers the punishment of his own unguarded speeches; or rather the man who hath foolish lips, shall be beaten, ver. 13.

10:9.text: Sincerely. Or Simply, Hebrew, "in uprightness," (Haydock) or innocence.
Manifest. The hypocrite shall be at last detected.

10:10.text: Sorrow. Septuagint add, "to men as well as to himself."

"But he who chides boldly shall make peace," (Haydock) or "work safety," as the Syriac and Arabic also read, instead of Hebrew, "a prating fool shall fall." "When a man connives at his friend's failings,...the offender is encouraged to sin on, and to heap up matter for very sorrowful reflections; but the man, who with an honest freedom, prudently reproves him, most effectually contrives his honour and safety." The consequences of a virtuous and a vicious friendship, seem to be also expressed in the next verse. Thus the latter hemistic generally illustrates the first. But here, part of ver. 8. may be improperly inserted. The two parts of the verses in Proverbs, etc., being arranged in distinct columns, has occasioned sometimes a part, and sometimes a whole verse, to be omitted, as the transcriber might mistake the line.

10:11.text: Life. Or a never-failing spring, fons perennis, as we should speak in Latin, Proverbs 13:14., and Apocalypse 7:17.

10:12.text: Sins. Septuagint, "all who contend." Charity pardons all, 1 Peter 4:8.

10:13.text: Sense. Literally, "a heart." But the Hebrews use this expression in a different sense from what we do, and thus designate a fool, Osee 7:11.

10:14.text: Confusion. He speaks inconsiderately, and involves himself in continual dangers, while the wise are cautious in their speech.

10:15.text: Poverty. Diffidence hinders the advancement of the poor, as presumption is too common among the rich. A happy mediocrity is best, ver. 16.

10:16.text: Life. In abundance he is not puffed up; but the wicked make use of their fruit or revenue to do evil. Their works are bad, unless they turn to God by at least an initial love of justice.

10:18.text: Foolish. We must neither dissemble our resentment, through hypocrisy, nor manifest it without reason.

10:19.text: Sin. A prolix discourse on subjects of importance is not reprehended.
But it is very difficult to speak much, without going against some virtue.

10:23.text: Man. He is enabled to see the evil of sin, and to avoid it, Job 15:16.

10:26.text: Him. He spoils all their projects, (Calmet) and becomes a nuisance.

10:29.text: Evil. Conscience upbraids them, and punishment is before their eyes. (Haydock) Magna vis est conscientiae.

10:30.text: Earth. This the Jews frequently experienced. The more enlightened understood, that such promises regarded also eternity.

11:1.text: Balance. Both in commerce, (Deuteronomy 25:13.; Calmet) and in passing sentence on others.

11:2.text: Wisdom. God resists the proud, Proverbs 16:18., and 18:12., and James 4:6.

11:7.text: Solicitous. Or ambitious. Hebrew, "the potent," or Septuagint, "the impious."

11:8.text: For him. As comparatively nothing worth to his master, Proverbs 21:18., and Isaias 43:3

11:11.text: Overthrown. Ten just men would have saved Sodom. Achan alone threw all Israel into confusion.

11:12.text: We must put up with some faults, as none are without.

11:13.text: Walketh. Septuagint, "the double-tongued," dissembler, or great talker.

11:15.text: That is. Hebrew, "that hateth those who make agreements is secure."

11:16.text: Gracious. Virtuous and beautiful, as God hath granted beauty also for good purposes. This and virtue tend to the advancement of women, while men can use their strength to acquire riches.

Glory. Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic add, "of her husband. But she that hateth righteousness is a throne of disgrace. The slothful, though rich, shall come to poverty; but the laborious shall retain their riches." Two hemistics seem to be lost in Hebrew.
Most of the additions in the Septuagint are only glosses, or useless repetitions, (Calmet) though they seem not to be so in this place.

11:17.text: Kindred. Hebrew, "flesh," Genesis 29:14. The miser is cruel even to himself.

11:21.text: In hand. At rest, or making agreements. God will punish the race of the wicked.

11:22.text: Foolish. Beauty, without prudence, leads to ruin, as ornaments are ill bestowed on swine. The women in the east sometimes wore rings in their noses, (Genesis 24:22.; Calmet) or hanging down upon them, Isaias 3:21.

11:24.text: Others. Moderation is always requisite. Hebrew, "there is one withholding from rectitude, yet for a defect," (Mont.[Montanus?]) being too saving, he is a loser.
Avarice does not always increase riches.

11:25.text: Himself. He shall receive abundantly, 2 Corinthians 9:6. The beneficent shall be amply rewarded both in this world and in the next.

11:26.text: Corn. In times of scarcity. See Amos 8:7.

11:29.text: House. By his profligacy, or law-suits, shall be impoverished, (ver. 17.; Calmet) or if he act with violence, he will make his house empty, Ecclesiasticus 4:35.

11:30.text: Life. Producing excellent fruits of virtue and edification.

11:31.text: Receive. Punishment, for almost inevitable faults, or be treated according to his deserts. Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic, "if the just be hardly saved, where shall the impious and the sinner appear?"
Afflictions attend the just in this life. Shall the wicked escape?

12:1.text: Knowledge. It is a great kindness to shew us our faults. But God's grace is necessary to make us reap benefit from correction, (Calmet) as self-love recoils at it.

12:2.text: But. Hebrew, "and he will condemn the man of devices," (Mont.[Montanus?]; Haydock) or, "the man of thoughts doth wickedly," (Calmet) as he trusts in them, rather than in God.

12:4.text: Diligent. Hebrew, "strong or virtuous," (Haydock) including all the perfections of the sex, and in particular those of economy and chastity, Proverbs 14:1., and 31:10.

12:7.text: Turn. In a moment the wicked is not to be found, Proverbs 10:25., and Psalm 36:35.

12:8.text: Learning. We apply to those things which we love, and those who study sacred (Calmet) or useful sciences, shall receive praise.

12:9.text: Glorious. Or a boaster, (Haydock) as many noblemen are, who are involved in debt, Ecclesiasticus 10:30.
It is better to have a sufficiency, than to be of noble parentage; and starving through a stupid idea, that work would be disgraceful.

12:10.src: Calmet; St. Chrysostom in Romans xxix.
12:10.text: Beasts. Those who treat them with cruelty, would do the like with men. God gives regulations to let brute beasts have rest, Leviticus 22:28.

12:11.text: Idleness. Hebrew, "the idle." Their company is seducing.
He that, etc. This occurs in the Septuagint, but not in the Hebrew or the new edition of St. Jerome.
Holds. Soldiers have thus been often surprised.
"Drunkenness is an incitement to lust and madness, the poison of wisdom."

12:12.text: Men. They wish to supplant one another.

12:13.text: Lips. Liars often become the victims of their own deceit.

12:16.text: Wise. It is more difficult to repress, than to avoid anger.
To dissemble, so as to seek an opportunity of revenge, is not commended.

12:17.text: That. Hebrew, "the truth announceth justice." We easily give credit to an honest man.

12:18.text: Promiseth. Septuagint, "there are, who speaking, wound with the sword; but," etc. Hebrew bote (Haydock) means also, making a foolish promise, which causes remorse.
This was the case with Herod, when he was pleased with Herodias, Matthew 14:8.
Hebrew, "speaketh like the piercings of a sword," (Protestants; Haydock) as detractors, and those who disseminate impious and scandalous maxims do.

12:19.text: Frameth. He studies how to escape detection. Hebrew, "a lying tongue is but for a moment;" it is presently discovered.

12:20.text: Honi soit qui mal y pense: "let him be covered with shame who thinks evil in it," seems nearly of the same import.

12:21.text: Sad. Even if he fall into sin, he will not lose all hope.
The accidents accompanying this life will not overwhelm him.
Hebrew, "no evil shall befall the just." If he be afflicted here, he will be amply rewarded hereafter. Septuagint, "the just will not be pleased with any injustice."

12:23.text: Cautious. Versutus is taken in a good, as well as in a bad sense. The wise are reserved in speaking, Proverbs 16:14.

12:25.text: Grief. Septuagint, "a fearful speech troubleth the heart of a (just) man."

12:26.text: Just. A true friend will make any sacrifice.

"I am convinced that friendship can subsist only among the good," says Cicero. Hebrew, "the just hath more, (Calmet; Protestants) or is more excellent than his neighbour." Septuagint, "the intelligent just is his own friend; (but the sentences of the impious are contrary to equity. Evils shall pursue sinners) but the way," etc.

12:27.text: Gain. Hebrew and Septuagint, "his prey," (Calmet) or what "he took in hunting."

12:28.text: Bye-way. Of vice. Hebrew, "and a way which leadeth to death," or "its paths conduct to death."

13:1.text: Doctrine. Or he gives proof of his good education, (Calmet) and excites even his father to advance in piety.
Septuagint, "is obedient to his father; but the disobedient son is in destruction."

13:2.text: Mouth. In reward of his good speeches.
Septuagint, "of justice the good man eateth; but the souls of the lawless perish before the time," Proverbs 12:13.

13:3.text: Hath. Hebrew, "who openeth his lips inconsiderately, is lost, Judges 11:35.

13:4.text: Willeth not. Hebrew, "hath not." He only desires riches, or to be converted, and will not labour, Proverbs 21:25., and Ecclesiasticus 5:8.

13:5.text: Confounded. The detractor is like swine, stirring up dirt.
The liar is not believed, even when he speaks the truth.

13:6.text: Sinner. Symmachus, "draweth on sin." Virtue is the best safeguard.

13:7.text: Riches. Such was St. Paul, 2 Corinthians 6:10. Some affect to be rich, while others are never satisfied. Semper avarus eget. Lazarus was very rich in God's sight.

13:8.text: Reprehension. Or is not able to defend himself, like the rich.
He is not exposed so much to great revolutions.

13:9.text: Out. They are hated as well as their offspring.

13:10.text: "Pride is the mother of all sects."

13:11.text: Haste. Hebrew, "by vanity," and injustice. Those who become rich on a sudden fall under suspicion, as a Roman objected to Sylla, who had inherited nothing.
By little. Hebrew, "he that gathereth by labour, (Septuagint, piety) shall increase."

13:12.text: Hope. Septuagint, "(the just shews mercy and lends) better is he who begins heartily to assist, than he who promises and leads to hope. For a bad (Grabe substitutes good) desire is a tree of life."
The pain increases in proportion to our eager desire. Calvin maintains, that the souls of the blessed are not yet in heaven, but hope: and of course he would establish a sort of purgatory for them.

13:13.text: Come. To defend what he has asserted, or to pass for a liar. Hebrew, "shall be destroyed by it."
Those who despise God's order shall perish.
Deceitful, etc. This is not in Hebrew, nor in some of the Latin editions.
Septuagint, "to the deceitful son nothing shall be good. But the ways of the wise servant shall prosper, and his paths shall be made straight," Proverbs 14:15.

13:14.text: That. Septuagint, "but the fool is slain in the snare."

13:15.text: Grace. God assists those who strive to be well instructed.

13:17.text: Of the. Hebrew, "a wicked ambassador." A king generally chooses people like himself.
Septuagint have read melec. "A rash king shall," etc.

13:18.text: To him. Hebrew, "destroy discipline," so that the most wretched are often noted for immorality. Septuagint, "instruction takes away poverty," as "the whole earth supports the man that has a trade," technion, according to the Greek proverb.

13:19.text: That is. Septuagint, "of the pious, but the works of the impious are far from knowledge."

13:20.text: Become. Septuagint, "be known." A person's disposition may be seen by the company which he frequents.

13:22.text: Heirs. This was more observable under the old law: yet we often see the distress to which the unjust are exposed. De male quaesitis non gaudet tertius haeres.

13:23.text: Fathers. Heirs often lose their property by their misconduct. Hebrew and (Haydock) Chaldean read, "of the poor," who till their land better than those who have too large farms.
Septuagint, "the just shall spend many years in affluence: but the unjust are cut off at once."

13:24.text: Betimes. God has always treated his friends in this manner, to preserve them from sin, or to increase their reward.

14:1.text: House. Giving her children a proper education, and taking care of her house, Proverbs 12:4., and Titus 2:5.

14:2.text: Is. Hebrew, "but the perverse in his ways despiseth him;" (Haydock) shewing by his conduct that he cares not for the Lord.
Septuagint, "he shall be dishonoured, that," etc.

14:3.text: Pride. He chastiseth with haughtiness, and is ever quarrelling.

14:4.text: Empty. As the land has not bee cultivated.
"The virtue of the preachers is manifested where there are many converted to produce fruit."

14:6.text: Not. Because they seek it ill, like the pagan sages. Septuagint, "thou shalt seek wisdom among the wicked, and shalt not find it," etc.

14:7.text: Prudence. Thou wilt presently perceive his weakness. Hebrew, "abandon a," etc. He is not capable of hearing reason: keep at a distance.

14:8.text: Way. This science of the saints is the only true wisdom.
Erreth. They are inconstant. Hebrew, "is deceit." They are bent on it.

14:9.text: Sin. Proverbs 10:23. Hebrew, "excuse sin," (Calmet) or "mock at sin," (Haydock) committed by others.
Grace, or good-will. They are agreeable to all.

14:10.text: Stranger. Such cannot well comfort the afflicted. A man is alone acquainted with the affections of his own heart. Septuagint, "he mixeth not insult" (Symmachus) "with strangers."

14:12.text: Death. How many, under the garb of piety, follow their passions! How many are misled by their singularity, or by unskilful directors!
We must suspect our own judgment.

If any Turks, Jews, or heretics, lead a moral good life, it seemeth both to themselves and to other ignorant people that they are in the right way to salvation; but their error in faith leadeth them to eternal damnation.
The persecutors thought they did God a service by putting the apostles to death. Will they be excused?

14:13.text: Laughter. Septuagint, "with content sorrow is not mixed."
Joy. Such is the condition of earthly things.

14:14.text: Above him. Septuagint, "with his counsels," enjoying the content of a good conscience, and a heavenly reward; while the wicked, with all his self-approbation, shall be punished.

14:15.text: Innocent. Good, and unsuspecting; (Josue 9:14., and 1 Corinthians 13:7.) or rather credulous, 1 John 4:1.
Septuagint, "the man who is not bad."
No, etc. This occurs (chap. 13:13.) in several copies: but here it is omitted in Hebrew, etc.

14:17.text: Folly. Wrath betrays us into great extravagances.
Hateful. Hebrew also, "hateth" folly. Septuagint, "the prudent beareth much," Job 5:2.

14:20.text: Many. "Riches make friends, poverty tries them."
False friends resemble swallows, which retire at the approach of winter.

14:21.text: He....mercy, is not found in Hebrew, Greek, or Latin manuscripts.

14:22.text: Truth. Those who are kind and faithful.
Septuagint add, (Calmet) as a second version, (Grabe) "the workers of evils understand not mercy and truth: but kind and faithful actions are with those who do good."

14:24.text: Imprudence. This they always betray, while the wise use their riches to assist their fellow-creatures, and receive a crown of glory.

14:28.text: King. Who formerly was styled "a shepherd," to remind him of the care with which he ought to seek the welfare of his subjects.

14:30.text: Bones. As a sound heart preserves the rest of the body, so a good intention often excuses from mortal sin, when the error is not gross. But envy corrupts the works which seem good, and which cannot bear a strict examination.
Septuagint, "a too sensible heart is the," etc. This is beautiful; but not quite conformable to the Hebrew.

14:31.text: Him. God takes the poor under his special protection, (Matthew 25:40.) and is the distributor of all riches. What would the rich do without the poor?

14:33.text: And. Protestants, "but that which is in the midst of fools is made known."
A vessel full of gold makes no noise, while that which contains only a few pieces sounds much.
Thus the fool makes a parade of all that he knows.
Septuagint, "but in the heart of fools, it is not known."
Aquila and Theodotion have the negation, (Calmet) as well as the Syriac and Arabic. See Proverbs 5:16.

14:34.text: Miserable. This sentence ought to be engraven in all public places. Hebrew, "and mercy the sinful people," whom God spares on account of their alms-deeds, (Daniel 4:24.) or "sin is the shame of peoples."

Montanus renders chesed mercy, and Pagnin "ignominy." The former is scarcely intelligible, et misericordia populorum peccatum, unless sin be here taken for a sin-offering, (Haydock) as it is by Vatable, Grotius, etc.

14:35.text: Nothing. Literally, "useless," which often means bad.
A servant who does not discharge his duty is such. Hebrew, "he that causeth to blush," and has no economy.
Septuagint, "He removes shame by his good management. Anger destroys the prudent; but a mild," etc.

15:1.text: Fury. This was seen in Nabal and Roboam, while Gideon and Abigail shewed the good effects of a mild answer, Judges 8:1., and 1 Kings 10:25., and 3 Kings 12:11.

15:2.text: Knowledge. It requires to be proposed in a proper manner.

15:4.text: Life. This comparison was become proverbial for something very excellent. Immoderate. Hebrew, "perverse."
Evil discourse kills the souls both of those who speak, and of those who pay attention to it.

15:5.text: In abundant, etc. Is omitted in Hebrew, Chaldean, and many Greek and Latin copies. The same idea is expressed in the following verse.

15:7.text: Unlike. The wise, or themselves. Hebrew and Septuagint, "inconstant."

15:8.text: Wicked. Still unconverted, Ecclesiasticus 34:23. When they repent, and offer sacrifice with sincerity, they will obtain mercy. The Donatists abused this text, to prove that baptism conferred by wicked ministers was unavailing. But St. Augustine shewed them, that Christ was the principal agent, and conferred grace, even though the minister might draw down judgments on his own head.

Even pagans confessed, that sacrifices, without virtue, could afford no benefit. "God was more pleased with the prayers of the Lacedemonians, than with all the sacrifices of the Greeks," say the idolatrous priests in Plato, Alcib. 2:Seneca de Ben. 1:5.

15:10.text: The mind which gives way to vice, must have embraced some false doctrine.

15:11.text: Before. Naked. The state of the damned is known to God, Proverbs 9:18.

15:13.text: Down. Compunction is salutary, but worldly grief blameable, Proverbs 25:20., and 2 Corinthians 7:10.

15:15.text: Feast. Those who repine at their condition are miserable; while the poor may be delivered from many cares.
-----O vitae tuta facultas Pauperis angustique laris.
Under affliction every thing displeases; but all delights the cheerful temper.

15:16.text: Content. Septuagint, "fear." Hebrew, "with trouble," which is preferable, 1 Timothy 6:6.

15:17.text: Calf. Or feast after sacrifice, 1 Kings 17:19., and Luke 15:23.

15:19.text: Thorns. All seems to them impossible.
In agriculture, however, (Haydock) "in domestic affairs, negligence is attended with more labour than diligence."

15:21.text: Fool. He thinks himself the most happy of mortals.
Septuagint, "the ways of the fools are senseless."

15:22.text: Established. We put in execution without fear, what has been resolved maturely.

15:24.text: One path leads to the realms above.
The wise must be intent on God.

15:26.text: Shall by, etc., is not added in Hebrew, etc., nor in several Latin manuscripts.

15:27.text: Troubleth. With all sorts of temporal misfortunes. A judge who receives bribes, is accursed, Exodus 23:8.
By, etc., is not here in Hebrew, but [in] Proverbs 16:6. St. Cyprian (3 Test.) found it in his copy.
Faith, or fidelity and truth, as it is elsewhere expressed, Proverbs 3:3. These virtues move God to pardon.

15:28.text: Studieth. And speaks deliberately. Roman Septuagint is more embarrassed in the rest of this, and the nine first verses of the following chapter, than the Complutensian edition.

15:30.text: Fat. And contributes to health. By less noble motives, the mind is gradually raised to the more excellent.

15:33.text: Lesson. Or "what teacheth." Humility, or docility, as those who acknowledge no master will never become wise, Matthew 23:12.

16:1.text: It is the part of man, etc. That is, a man should prepare in his heart and soul what he is to say; but after all, it must be the Lord that must govern his tongue, to speak to the purpose. Not that we can think any thing of good without God's grace: but after that we have (with God's grace) thought and prepared within our souls what we would speak; if God does not govern our tongue, we shall not succeed in what we speak.
He will put into our mouths what we have to say to persecutors, Luke 21:14. He often causes us to utter the reverse of what we intended, as Balaam did, Numbers xxiii.

The fairest prospects miscarry without God's blessing. The enemies of grace would infer from this text, that the beginning of salvation depends on free-will. But St. Augustine (con. 2. epist. Pelag. 2:8.) has solidly refuted them, and Solomon does not mean that man acts alone, Proverbs 8:35., (Septuagint) John 15:5., and 2 Corinthians 3:5. "Man," says St. Augustine, "does no good things, which God does not cause him to perform."
The Scripture cannot contradict itself. A fresh grace is requisite to execute what God has enabled us to devise, ver. 9.

16:2.text: Hebrew, "pure in his own eyes." He sees not his own defects, Proverbs 21:2., and Job 28:23.

16:3.text: Open. Hebrew, "roll on," and refer all to God's glory.

16:4.text: Day. His obduracy is of his own choice, and must serve to set the divine justice in the clearest light, Ecclesiasticus 33:14., and Exodus 9:16. Others hence infer that predestination is gratuitous, and reprobation in consequence of sin. It seems rather that temporal goods and evils are here meant.

16:5.text: Hand. And he seems to be very quiet, Proverbs 11:21. Septuagint, "but he who putteth his hand in hands unjustly, to make a contract, is," etc.
The, etc., is taken from the Roman Septuagint and occurs before, Proverbs 15:27.

16:6.text: Mercy to the distressed, Proverbs 3:3., and 14:22.

16:7.text: Peace. Thus Jacob, Joseph, Daniel, etc., were admired by their former enemies.

16:10.text: Judgment. Or "let it not err," as people look upon the decisions of kings as so many oracles. We ought to act in this manner, as long as they are not visibly unjust. God gave a principal spirit (Psalm 50:14.) to Saul, David, Solomon, and to the judges whom he appointed, 1 Kings 10:9., Deuteronomy 34:9., and Judges 3:10.
Solomon was thus enabled to decide difficult cases.

16:11.text: Bag. Many read saeculi, "of the world." So Ven. Bede, etc. All God's appointments are perfectly just, Proverbs 11:21. It was the custom for people to carry balances to weigh money, before it was coined.

16:13.text: Loved. Yet none are more exposed to flattery and deceit than kings.

16:15.text: Life. A mild government resembles a serene sky.

16:16.text: Get. Septuagint, "the nests of wisdom....and the nests of prudence;" or Churches of Christ, or places of education, may be intended.

16:18.text: Fall. Our first parents had given way to pride, before they sinned publicly.

16:21.text: Shall. Hebrew, "adds learning," both to himself and to others. Those who are wise and eloquent, must be preferred before those who have only the former qualification.

16:26.text: Mouth. The want of food, Ecclesiastes 6:7.

16:28.text: Words. Protestants, "a whisperer separateth chief friends."

16:30.text: Lips. These motions indicate fury and pensiveness.

16:31.text: Justice. To the just longevity is promised.

16:32.text: Valiant. Alexandrian Septuagint adds, "and a prudent man than a great farmer." Greek: Georgiou.
Cities. To govern the passions is more difficult. (St. Gregory, Past. 3:p. Adm. x.; St. Thomas Aquinas 2:2. q. 128. a. 6.) Latius regnes avidum domando Spiritum, quam si Lybiam, etc.

16:33.text: Lord. So the apostles had recourse to them, (Acts 1:26.) as the Cophts[Copts?] and Nestorians still do when there is a dispute about the election of a patriarch.
This mode may settle disputes, Proverbs 18:18. But we must not have recourse to it, except where the Church permits, lest we become the dupes of an idle curiosity.
Septuagint, "all things come into the breast of the unjust; but all just things proceed from the Lord."

17:1.text: Victims. Of which part was used for a feast, Proverbs 7:14.
Septuagint add, "of many goods, and unjust victims."

17:2.text: Brethren. Partaking with them, (Abenezra; Tirinus; Menochius) or rather acting as his master's executor; which evinces the advantages of wisdom, so as to raise a slave above those whom his station requires him to serve. Such was Joseph, who was tried.

17:4.text: Lips. They mutually deceive one another, as well as others.

17:6.text: Fathers. Whose virtues they are bound to imitate.

17:7.text: Eloquent. Septuagint, "faithful." They cannot be expected from them.

17:8.text: Expecteth heavenly things. Septuagint, "instruction is the reward of good deeds to those who use it."

17:9.text: Friends. Detractors cause dissensions, and even wars.

17:10.text: Fool. "A word is enough for the wise." Nobilis equus umbra virgae regitur, ignavus ne calcari quidem.

17:11.text: Angel. Messenger of death, (Menochius) which is sometimes inflicted by good, at others by bad, angels of death, or satan, 2 Corinthians 12:7., and Psalm 77:49.

17:12.text: Fool. Hebrew, "fool in his folly." The danger is not greater in meeting (Calmet) a female bear, though it be the most terrible.

17:14.text: Water. It is scarcely possible to prevent the bad effects of detraction. Frequent quarrels have also risen on account of springs, Genesis 26:20.
Judgment. And gives up the cause, as being much more prudent, Matthew 5:25, 40.

17:15.text: God. We ought to suspend our judgment, or incline to the more favourable side, Matthew 7:1., and Romans 2:1.

17:16.text: He, etc., is not here in Hebrew, though it be equivalently (ver. 19.) where the Septuagint are silent.

17:17.text: Distress. Like the real friend, Proverbs 18:24.

17:18.text: Hands. Through joy, or as a mark of his consent.

17:19.text: Door. Sixtus V reads, "mouth:" as some explain the door to mean. A large door supposes that the house is elevated, and thus exposed to danger from winds, etc.

17:24.text: Earth. Wandering and insatiable, and deeming the acquisition of wisdom too difficult.

17:26.text: No good. But very criminal. See Jeremias 7:31.

17:27.text: Precious and reserved spirit. This is a mark of wisdom.

18:1.text: Reproach. True friendship resembles charity, and bears all things, 1 Corinthians 13:4. Hebrew now reads Thave, "desire;" instead of Thuane, occasion, or "pretext," which must have been in the copies of the Septuagint and Vulgate.
Protestants, "through desire, a man having separated himself, seeketh and intermeddleth with all wisdom." The solitary seeks heaven.

18:2.text: Hebrew, "unless to lay open his heart." He wishes to appear wise, and to justify his wicked designs.

18:3.text: Contemneth both God and man, Luke 18:4. Hebrew, "is contemned" in his turn.
He is like a man abandoned by the physician.

18:4.text: Man, who is just and wise. His advice deserves attention, Proverbs 20:5.

18:6.text: Quarrels. Hebrew, "blows." Septuagint, "death;" (ver. 7.) which are the usual consequences of quarrels.

18:8.text: Tongued. Hebrew, "calumniator." He pretends to wish well to those of whom he speaks, or else to guard the company against deceit.
"If the devil be upon the detractor's tongue, he is in the ears of those who pay attention to him."
Fear, etc., is in the Septuagint, above. The Vulgate retains both this and the new version of St. Jerome.

18:9.text: Brother. Like him, as both end in poverty, Proverbs 10:4., and 12:11.

18:10.text: Name. Essence, or protection of God. The rich depends on his own wealth.

18:13.text: Heareth the end of the question, Proverbs 1:5.

18:14.text: That is. Theodotion, "is wounded, who shall support?"

18:16.text: Princes. He easily finds access by showing submission. (Calmet) Placatur donis Jupiter ipse suis.

18:17.text: Him. To see that he act with sincerity.

18:19.text: Judgments of many are more deserving of credit. Hebrew, "a brother offended, is like a strong place, and their quarrels," etc. They are not easily reconciled. Civil wars are most terrible.

18:20.text: Satisfied. Those who are guarded in their words get employment.

18:21.text: Love it, and speak well or ill, shall receive accordingly, Matthew 12:37.

18:22.text: Good wife. Good is not in Hebrew, but should be understood, as it is expressed in the Complutensian (Calmet) and Alexandrian Septuagint.
He that, etc., occurs not in Hebrew, Sixtus V, etc. But it is found in Septuagint and Arabic. The Syriac omits the last sentence.
Wicked. St. Augustine had frequently asserted that a divorce was only of counsel: but this he retracted, when he reflected on this text.

18:22.src: Selden, Ux. 3:16.; Dem. in Neaeram, etc.
18:22.text: The Hebrews, Athenians, and Romans, followed the same practice with adulteresses.

Hermas (past. 1:4.) prescribes that the penitent shall be received again, but not often. In case of divorce, the fathers still permit not a second marriage, that the parties may be reconciled. They enjoin the husband to put away only such as are incorrigible.

18:24.text: Brother. The ties of nature are not so strong as those of friendship.
Hebrew, "a man that hath friends must shew himself friendly; and there is a friend that sticketh closer than a brother."

19:1.text: Rich, is not in Hebrew, Septuagint, Complutensian, or St. Jerome.

But as there is no antithesis between a poor man and a fool, it ought to be inserted, as it is in the Syriac, which reads, "than he who is perverse in his ways, though he be rich." The Manuscript 2 confirms "ways."

19:2.text: Soul. Wholesome and spiritual. Hebrew, "the soul or life is not good."
Stumble. "The more I make haste, the less I advance," said Plato; (Republic 7.) and Augustus often repeated; Sat cito, si sat bene.

19:3.text: Fretteth. Literally, "burneth." Septuagint, "blameth." Hebrew, "his heart rageth against the Lord."
Thus Adam tacitly laid the blame on him, as all those do who excuse themselves on account of their temper, habits, stars, the violence of temptation, etc.

19:7.text: Only. Hebrew has lo, and the margin lu, with the Vulgate.

The former word intimates that the words have no solidity, and the latter that they shall be his only portion who is so foolish as to trust in them. Sequens dicta non ispa; (Montanus) or ei ipsa, if we follow the margin.

19:8.text: Loveth. This does not contradict the gospel; (John 12:25.) as those who refrain from what the soul would crave to its own detriment, truly love it.

19:10.text: Fool. He would eat them, so as to prejudice his health.
Princes. Proverbs 30:21. Such are commonly insolent, and should attend to Ausonius. Fortunam reverenter habe.

19:11.text: Wrongs. The wise man is not vindictive or punctilious.

19:12.text: Anger. Is not their dominion enough? Having none to admonish them, they do not perceive their excesses.

19:13.text: Through. It cannot be endured long.

19:14.text: Hence the Fathers dissuade marrying with infidels.

19:15.text: Non progredi, regredi est. St. Bernard says, "Not to advance, is to go back."

19:16.text: Neglecteth. Hebrew, "contemneth." In the law of Moses, many transgressions are punished with death, but here that of the soul is meant.

19:17.text: Such usury is lawful, and God will abundantly reward acts of mercy.
He engages his word for the poor.

19:18.text: Killing. Protestants, "crying;" or by his complaint be not deterred.

Chaldean agrees with us. The law permitted parents to sell their children, and to have them stoned to death, if they declared them disobedient, (Deuteronomy 21:18.; Calmet) and riotous, ver. 20. Timely chastisement may prevent such extremities.
St. Paul dissuades unnecessary severity, Colossians 3:21.

19:19.text: Thing. A child neglected will continue to steal, or to offend; (Calmet) while too much harshness will do no good, but tend to the damage of all parties.
Anger is productive of the worst effects.

19:21.text: Firm. Ignorance and weakness cause men to change.

19:22.text: Merciful. Hebrews 2:17. Non ignara mali miseris succurrere disco.
Hebrew, "the desire of a man is his mercy," (Montanus; Haydock) or "shame," (R. Levi) as it leads to sin, Genesis 6:5. All wish to be esteemed merciful.
Man of quality. Hebrew meish, "proe viro."

19:23.text: Life. Both temporal and eternal, Proverbs 10:27.
Sixtus V, "by the most wicked one." The devil shall have no power over him.

19:24.text: Arm-pit. Or Hebrew tsallachath, "in the pot," out of which he eats; which shews his negligence.
Mouth, to eat. This hyperbole indicates that he is too lazy to take the necessary sustenance, Proverbs 26:15.
Septuagint, "in his breast unjustly. Neither will he bring them to his mouth."
He will bestow nothing, and shall reap no benefit from his avarice.
He will not practise what he teaches.

19:25.text: Wicked. Hebrew, "scoffer." Chastisements have always some good effect either on the sufferer, or on the spectators, who will be cautioned not to imitate what is wrong.

19:26.text: Infamous. Hebrew, "a son of confusion and reproach;" a spendthrift.

19:27.text: Not. Hebrew seems to say the reverse.

But we may read it with an interrogation. "Wilt thou cease?" etc. Or the instruction here meant is of a dangerous nature. Protestants, "that causeth to err from the words of knowledge." Septuagint, "a son who neglects to keep the instruction of his father, shall muse on bad sayings."

20:1.text: A luxurious thing. Hebrew, "a scoffer." Chaldean, "renders one a scoffer."

Drunkenness. Hebrew ssocor, any strong drink, particularly palm-wine. Intemperance places the strongest obstacles in the way of wisdom. It causes a person to mock at all sacred things, and to be quarrelsome, Proverbs 23:29., and Ephesians 5:18.

20:3.text: Reproaches. It is better not to commence a lawsuit, even when we are in the right, Proverbs 17:11.
Plena victoria est ad clamantem tacere.

20:5.text: Out. So David discovered the meaning of the Thecuite, 2 Kings 14:18. A wise politician carefully examines everything in a foreign court.

20:6.text: Faithful. Few continue steady to their engagements or friends, whom they will assist to a certain point. In God these two virtues always go together, Psalm 84:11. But they are rarely found in men.

20:8.text: Look. It is the duty of kings to administer justice.

20:9.text: Sin. Protestants, "my sin?" We know not when it is remitted.

20:9.src: Bayn.; St. Augustine in Psalm cxlix.
20:9.text: Without a special revelation, no one can be secure, 1 John 1:8., and Ecclesiastes 9:1.

20:10.text: Measures. In commerce, (Calmet) as well as in judging.

20:11.text: Right. We may form some judgment of his future conduct, from the inclinations which he manifests in his infancy. Naturam expellas furca, tamen ipsa recurret, Et mala perrumpet furtim fastidia victrix.

20:12.text: Both. Consequently he will know all our actions, Psalm 93:9. We must refer all to him, as he gives us the means of learning.

20:13.text: Sleep. Septuagint, "back-biting, that thou mayst not be taken off."

20:14.text: Buyer. This is the common practice; yet it is not without exceptions. St. Augustine (Trin. 13:3.) observes, that the mountebank having promised to tell what every person had in his heart, many came to the theatre, when he told them that they all wished to buy cheap, and to sell dear. They all applauded the remark.

20:16.text: Strangers. For whom he has bound himself foolishly, Proverbs 6:1. All who have the care of others, must answer for them.

20:17.text: Lying. Deceit, and unlawful pleasures, Proverbs 9:17. But God mingles disgust with them, and will punish the guilty, at least hereafter. Worldly enjoyments seem sweet, but they are full of gravel, and hurtful.

20:18.text: Governments. Or prudence, else the best designs may prove abortive.

20:19.text: Lips. And speaketh much. These people are unworthy of our friendship.

20:21.text: Blessing. It is morally impossible that they should have been acquired justly, Proverbs 13:11., and 21:5.

20:22.text: Evil. And revenge myself. This belongs to the Lord, Deuteronomy 32:35. Man would be too favourable to himself, and would also pronounce his own condemnation, as he is also a sinner.

20:24.text: Way? Jeremias 10:23. Independently of God, who can do any good?

20:25.text: Ones. Hebrew, "the saint or holy thing."

Chaldean, "to make a vow for the sanctuary, and afterwards repent;" having acted inconsiderately at first. To attack the persons or relics of the saints, or to plunder what is consecrated to pious uses, will bring on destruction; so also to make vows, and then seek to evade them, will not pass unpunished.

20:26.text: He will make his enemies lie prostrate under his chariot-wheels, 2 Kings 12:31.

20:27.text: Lamp. The breath of life, (Genesis 2:7.) and the light of man, 1 Corinthians 2:11.

20:28.text: Clemency. Such a king need not fear rebellion.

20:29.text: Hairs, and experience. They have a greater contempt of death and pleasures.

20:30.text: Evils. The wicked shall derive benefit from correction.
Belly. They shall feel the remorse of conscience, as Chaldean seems to indicate.
A serious illness often causes people to repent.

21:1.text: It. Though it be free, and may resist grace or embrace virtue, Isaias 45:22., Jeremias 3:14., and 7:3., and Josue 24:23.
Yet God knows how to turn the heart even of a king, so as to preserve his liberty, with the same ease as a gardener brings the streams of water to his plants.

21:3.text: Mercy. Septuagint, "justice and truth." (Haydock) See Osee 6:6.

21:4.text: Heart. Or when it is proud, it causes the eyes to appear so too.

Lamp. Protestants, "ploughing of the wicked is sin." When it is done with a bad motive, out of pride. Hebrew, "the haughtiness of the eyes, and the enlarging of the heart, are the labour ("tillage," Montanus; "thought," Pagnin) of the wicked; yea, sin."
Those things are their employment, and their sin.
Septuagint and Vulgate may also mean, "sin is the lamp and exultation of the wicked."

21:5.text: Sluggard. Hebrew, "hasty man," as the slothful is ironically styled. He would see his desires accomplished without labouring; and will never stick to work. This levity is most injurious to his advancement.

21:6.text: Death. Their injustice shall be punished.

21:8.text: Strange. Unknown, impure way, Leviticus 10:1., and Deuteronomy 17:15.

21:9.text: Top, (domatis) as the roofs in Palestine were flat.
Any inconvenience had better be endured, than to live with the quarrelsome.

21:10.text: Neighbour. Symmachus, "he has no idea of friendship." Septuagint, "he shall not be treated with mercy by any man."

21:11.text: Knowledge. These are the effects of chastisements and good company.

21:12.text: Evil. He tries every expedient to save him.

21:14.text: They put all valuable things in the bosom.

21:15.text: To do judgment. Or to see it done, while the wicked depend only on the corruption of their judges.

21:16.text: Giants, in hell, chap 15:11., Job 26:5., and Ezechiel 22:21.

21:17.text: Those who squandered away their goods, were fined by the Areopagites.

21:18.text: Righteous. Many are deterred from wickedness by the punishment of malefactors, whose injuries are frequently irreparable. God abandoned Pharao and Aman to save his people. He treated them as victims of wrath, Isaias 43:3.

21:20.text: Treasure of provisions. Temporal blessings were promised as a figure of more lasting ones, which are set before Christians.

21:22.text: Thereof. Nothing can resist, Matthew 12:29., and Ecclesiastes 9:16.

21:24.text: Such a one is always actuated by pride, when he attempts to turn good advice and religion to ridicule.

21:25.text: At all. Hence his desires of riches and wisdom prove fruitless, Proverbs 13:4.

21:27.text: Wickedness. Hebrew, "with an evil thought." (Montanus) Ecclesiasticus 34:24.

21:28.text: Obedient. Attentive, faithful witness, shall speak with triumph, or he who obeys the law, shall gain the victory over the devil, etc.

21:29.text: Face. Never blushing, or yielding to good advice.

21:31.text: Battle. It was kept for no other purpose by the eastern nations, who used oxen to till the land; asses and camels to carry burdens, Job 39:18., and Psalm 19:8., and 32:17.

22:1.text: It is preferable to riches, but not to be compared with virtue, which is the only solid good; and even to be placed above riches, it must be well grounded.

22:2.src: St. Chrysostom, hom. xxxiv. in 1 Corinthians.
22:2.text: Another. They stand in need of one another.
They are equal in God's sight, who only values real virtue. He disposes of riches, so that the poor may one day become rich.

22:4.text: Fruit. Literally, "the end of modesty, (Haydock) or moderation, which must accompany every virtue.

22:5.text: Perverse. They are always in danger and in trouble.

22:6.text: It is a proverb, is added by St. Jerome, to make the sentence more striking.
It. He is like a tender plant, (Calmet) or wax, or a new vessel. Quo semel est imbuta recens servabit odorem Testa diu.
"Shall wool regain its whiteness after it has been dyed purple?"
Hebrew, "initiate a young," etc. Proportion your lessons to his capacity, and make him relish them.

22:7.text: Servant. He might be sold, etc., Exodus 22:3., and Matthew 18:25. (Gell. 20:1.) Plato (Leg. viii.) would have nothing sold on credit. These laws appear to be severe; but they are founded on wisdom, as nothing impoverishes more than the facility of borrowing.

22:8.text: Consumed. Or beaten with the flail of God's anger.

22:9.text: Is. Hebrew, "has a good eye," in opposition to the evil, or malicious one, Matthew 20:15.
He, etc., is not in Hebrew, or in the Latin edition of Comp. and St. Jerome.

22:11.text: He. Septuagint, "the Lord loveth pious hearts. All the irreproachable are acceptable to him. The king feeds with lips," by his just ordinances.
Kings hate duplicity; but require that the truth should be disclosed to them in a suitable manner.

22:15.text: Folly. Ignorance and innate corruption are corrected by a good education.

22:17.text: Incline. Thus Solomon concludes his discourse, (chap. 24:23.) in the same manner as he began it, to Proverbs 10. Some commence the third book of Proverbs in this place; others, Proverbs 25.

22:18.text: Lips. Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh.

22:20.src: Bossuet; Tirinus; 2 Corinthians 12:8., and Amos 1:11.; Calmet

22:20.text: Protestants, "have not I written to thee excellent things in counsels and knowledge?" "Shalishim," perfect, (Pagnin) or "three things," (Montanus) means also (Haydock) such as might suit princes and great officers.

22:21.text: Sent. Septuagint, "are sent to thee." Thou mayst become a teacher, (Haydock) or give satisfaction to thy parents, who have sent thee to my school.

22:25.text: Soul. By imitating him, or by falling a victim to his rage.

22:26.text: Such a one might be required to pay the debt, Proverbs 20:16.

22:28.text: Set. The pagans made a god of Terminus, to prevent disputes.
If it be unlawful to disturb land-marks, how much more so is it to give way to novelty in religion?
Solomon is addressing those who follow the true faith. Else the conduct of infidel ancestors should not deter any from embracing the truth.

22:29.text: Obscure. By industry he shall raise himself to notice.
Kings employ those who are most active.

23:1.text: Sit. Saul sat at table, and the custom of lying down was adopted only a little while before the captivity. It was recent among the Romans.

23:2.text: Throat. Restrain intemperance and talkativeness, Ecclesiasticus 31:12. Septuagint, "stretch forth thy hand, knowing that thou must prepare the like; but if thou be more insatiable, (3.) desire not his meats, for he has them of a deceitful life." They cannot afford real happiness, (Haydock) and to vie with the rich would only reduce them to poverty, Ecclesiasticus 13:2. St. Augustine (tr. xlvii. in Joan.) explains this text of the blessed Eucharist, observing, that we must give our life for our brethren, as Christ did for us. Before communion, we must slay the old man, and subdue our passions.
Power. Protestants, "if thou be a man given to appetite." The situation of a courtier is very critical.
Those who eat with the kings of Persia, were nicely observed by an eunuch, lest they should cast their eyes on any of his concubines.

23:3.text: Deceit. Poison. He wishes to discover thy secret.

23:4.text: Prudence. Be more solicitous for this, than to acquire riches.
Yet this wisdom must be sober, Romans 12:3., and 1 Timothy 6:9. Septuagint, "being poor, do not stretch forth thyself to the rich, but prudently retire,["] ver. 2.

23:5.text: Riches. Septuagint, "to him, the rich man, he no where appears. He has prepared," etc.
Like. Hebrew, "as the eagle, it will fly," etc.
We must therefore fix our hearts on more durable goods.

23:6.text: Man. Hebrew, "eat not bread of an evil eye," the envious, or rather the sordid miser.

23:7.text: Like. Protestants, "as he thinketh in his heart, so is he: eat," etc.

He is still convinced that his guests will ruin him: or "like one guarding, or trembling for his soul." Septuagint, "swallowing a hair, he saith," etc. He is afraid of expense, and would allow himself as little as possible.

Diviner. Such endeavour to speak what may come to pass, but are full of anxiety; so the miser's words are contrary to his real sentiments, (Calmet) as the diviner knows that he is imposing on mankind.

23:8.text: Words. Thou wilt be disgusted, and repine, Ecclesiasticus 31:25.

23:11.text: Kinsman. Hebrew Gaal, "tutor, defendant, or redeemer," the Lord (Haydock) himself, Leviticus 25:25.

23:18.text: Thou. Protestants, "surely there is an end." Marginal note, "reward."
The testimony of a good conscience affords the greatest comfort in death. Septuagint, "if thou observe these things, thou shalt have posterity." Hebrew, "hopes."

23:20.text: Eat. Such feasts tend to corrupt the morals, and to misspend time.

23:21.text: Rags. At death the indolent shall be exposed to shame.

23:23.text: Sell. Acquire as much wisdom as possible, and keep it with care.
Septuagint, "do not drive wisdom from thee."

23:27.text: Pit. It is difficult to overcome this passion, when once it has got possession of the heart. We must therefore watch over it, and consecrate it invariably to wisdom, ver. 26.

23:28.text: Him. Protestants, "increaseth the transgressors among men," (Haydock) and like a harpy, kills all whom she can entrap.

23:29.text: Whose father. St. Jerome has read ab avi, instead of abo, (Calmet) which is an interjection, (Bochart) alas! or it means, "trouble." Septuagint, "drunkenness," (Chaldean; Calmet) or "sorrow."
Falls. Septuagint, "hath sorrows." Hebrew, "babbling," (Protestants; Haydock) or discontents of mind.
Cause. Drunkards often fall upon their best friends, as Alexander did on Clytus.

23:31.text: Yellow. Or bright, as it is said there is only one red wine in Palestine.
Pleasantly. Hebrew, "it goeth right," and is excellent.

23:33.text: Women. Wine excites to lust. (Calmet) See Proverbs 20:1.

23:34.text: When. Septuagint, "in a great wave." Never is reason more wanted, nor less able to perform her duty.

23:35.text: Again. This is the woeful effect of drunkenness, that men are not deterred from it, though they be sensible of its dreadful consequences.

24:1.text: Like. Be not allured by their prosperity to imitate them, Psalm 36:1.

24:3.text: Wisdom and virtue, and not by injustice can the house be established.

24:5.text: Valiant, as well as a good economist, ver. 4.

24:6.text: Counsels. "Consult many what ought to be done, but only a few of the most faithful, or rather thyself alone, what thou art about to do."

24:7.text: High. Thus the fool excuses himself. But wisdom condescends to our weakness, if we be truly in earnest, Deuteronomy 32:12. Mouth. To defend himself, or to give advice.

24:9.text: Of a fool. In as much as he is wicked. Though he may have some pious thoughts, he attends not to them.
He thinks how he may commit evil, and renders himself hateful.
Hebrew, "a wicked thought is the sin of folly." Septuagint, "the fool dieth in sins."

24:10.text: Diminished. This is the sad consequences of too much dejection, Ephesians 4:19. Despairing, they abandon themselves to impurities.

24:11.text: Deliver. The Jews often put people to death without any formal trial, pretending zeal, as they did St. Stephen, etc. Our Saviour rescued the adulteress from such a situation, as Daniel had done Susanna. Yet this text may regard poor debtors, Psalm 71:4.

24:11.src: St. Ambrose in Psalm cxviii. Ser. viii. Off. 1:36., and ep. xxv., and xxvi.
24:11.text: Christian bishops used all their influence to preserve the lives of those who did not deserve death.

24:12.text: I have. Hebrew, "behold, we know not this man."
He is a stranger. But all mankind are brethren, and have a charge to assist one another, even though they be enemies, Ecclesiasticus 17:12., and Exodus 23:4.
Keeper. Hebrew notser, "preserver." As thou hast received many good things from God, shew mercy to thy neighbour.

24:13.text: Honey. Of wisdom, which is most delicious.

24:14.text: Thou shalt. Hebrew, "yea, it is the last."
Thou shalt enjoy old age, or have posterity.

24:16.src: Vatable; St. Augustine, City of God 11:31.
24:16.text: Fall into smaller sins, (St. Gregory vi. in 2 Reg. xv. etc.) or into disgrace, as yippol (Haydock) rather intimates.
Both significations agree with the context. See Job 5:27., and Matthew 18:21.

He who is not subject to mortal sin, may still be exposed to many failings, and venial sins, which do not deprive him of the title of just; whereas the wicked consents to mortal sin, from which he riseth not so easily. Hence the wise man admonishes us not to lie in wait, or calumniously seek impiety in the house or soul of the just.

24:18.text: Thus will thy thirst of vengeance be disappointed.

The Hebrews believed that there was no evil, which was not caused by sin; and this was true in some sense. But still God often afflicts his servants, (ver. 16,) as the whole book of Job tends to prove.

24:20.text: Come. Protestants, "no reward," (Haydock) posterity, etc., as designated also by the lamp, ver. 14.

24:21.text: Detractors. Or those who speak ill of God or the king.
Protestants, "that are given to change," (Haydock) and relapses.

24:23.text: These. Septuagint have an addition, and then our Proverbs 30:1-15., after which follows the remainder of this chapter and the ten first verses of the 31st. [chapter.]
Solomon here resumes the sententious style, Proverbs 22:17.

24:26.text: Lips. And be deemed a friend.

24:27.text: House, and support thy family. Before building, great deliberation is requisite, Luke 14:28. Those who attempt to instruct others, must first set good example.

24:28.text: Cause, and necessity. Septuagint, "be not a false witness against thy fellow-citizen."

24:29.text: Work. Revenge is often reprobated, though the law allowed of retaliation, which the more virtuous did not insist upon.

24:30.text: Man. Those who neglected their land were despised.

24:30.src: Cato, Rust. i.; Cicero, Off. i.
24:30.text: The ancient Romans esteemed agriculture as a most laudible and profitable employment.

24:32.text: Which. Septuagint, "at last I repented: I looked forward to receive instruction."

24:33.text: Said I, is not in Hebrew, Proverbs 6:10.
Septuagint, "I will slumber a while," to rest. Septuagint, "I will enfold my breast in my hands a little."

25:1.text: These. Solomon wrote 3,000, and we have only 915 verses extant.
The rest perhaps shewed his genius, but were less useful.
Out of other records, (Menochius) or "translated" into a language better understood.

25:2.text: Speech. The Scriptures will denounce the truth to them, and shew them how to reign with justice. We must adore the mysteries of God; but are allowed to examine the secret designs of princes.

25:3.text: Unsearchable. Their counsellors must not betray their secrets, Tobias 12:7. The greatest enterprises depend on secrecy.

25:5.text: Justice. The wicked in a kingdom resemble rust on silver.

25:6.text: We must not seek the first places, Luke 14:10. Vive sine invidia, mollesque inglorius annos Exige; amicitias et tibi junge pares.

25:8.text: Not. Septuagint, "repent when thy friend may reproach thee."
Friend. A word spoken in haste may expose him to ridicule.

25:9.text: Stranger. It sometimes happens that friends fall out; but if either disclose the secret of the other, he will be deemed infamous.
