St. Ambrose says of his brother Satyrus, "though we had all things in common, yet the secret of our friends was not so."

25:10.text: Grace, etc., is no in Hebrew, Complutensian, St. Jerome, etc. But it is in the Septuagint, "favour and friendship may give liberty; which keep thou for thyself, that thou mayst not be exposed to great shame. But guard thy ways unchangeably."

25:11.text: Time, (Symmachus) "on its wheels," (Hebrew) flowing smoothly, (Calmet) or "according to his two faces, is apples of gold in network of silver." The Scriptures have a double sense. The exterior one leads to that sense which is interior, and more excellent.
Beds. On such the kings of Parthia slept, and these metals were very common under Solomon, 3 Kings 10:27., and Esther 1:6.
Montanus renders mascioth "transparent cases." Protestants, "pictures of silver."

25:12.text: Bright. Hebrew chali cathem, "an ornament of fine gold," (Montanus; Protestants; Haydock) may probably denote a collar or ring. The eastern nations wore rings fixed at the top of the ears, and under the nose. Some were so large that they put their meat through them. The Scripture often alludes to these customs, which are so different from ours.

25:13.text: Harvest. In June and July, when the heat was most intense, people of quality had snow from Libanus to mix with what they drank, Jeremias 18:14.

25:16.text: Up. We must moderate the sensual appetite, (Menochius) and even the study of wisdom, which is compared to honey, Proverbs 24:13., Romans 12:3., and Ecclesiastes 7:17.
We must not be too familiar, ver. 17.

25:17.text: Having. Hebrew, "being tired of thee." No man is so perfect, but he will manifest some defect, and become importunate. (Calmet) Nulli te facias nimis sodalem.

25:20.text: And. Protestants, "as he that taketh away a, etc., and as vinegar....to a heavy heart."

The former sentence may be joined with the preceding, as it is improper to deprive a person of his garment, no less than to trust in the faithless; though some would suppose (Calmet) that this conduct, as well as the mixing of vinegar with nitre, is no less absurd than to attempt to relieve by music those who are extremely afflicted, Ecclesiasticus 22:6.

But Solomon does not speak of such, but only of those who are "heavy;" and we know that music has wonderful efficacy in relieving them, (1 Kings 16:17.) in like manner as this mixture serves to cleanse the skin and garments, (Jeremias 2:22.; Calmet) and to purify the ears, when they are deafish.
As a, etc., is not in Hebrew, St. Jerome, etc.
The Chaldean has the latter part, (Haydock) "grief tries the heart, as fire does silver. As the worm eats wood, so folly," etc.

25:22.text: Coals of charity; (St. Chrysostom in Romans 12:20.) or, if he prove obstinate, his punishment will be the greater.
The former sense is more received.

25:23.text: Rain; (Symmachus; Protestants) or marginal note, "bringeth forth rain."

But St. Jerome, who live din the country, knew that this wind was rather dry; and therefore he has abandoned the Septuagint, raiseth the clouds," Job 37:9., Joel 2:20., and Ecclesiasticus 18:23. The countries north of Palestine were not calculated to produce vapours and rain, which came rather from the south.

25:23.src: St. Jerome, ad. Rust.; St. Bernard
25:23.text: Tongue. If the hearers would shew their displeasure, detractors would soon be reduced to silence.

25:24.text: It is. Proverbs 21:9. Sixtus V does not insert this verse here.

25:25.text: Tidings. Hebrew and Septuagint. The Vulgate seems rather to speak of a "good messenger." Homer said that a good messenger honoured the business most.

25:26.text: Falling into disgrace, or sin, occasions the wicked to exult, as if there were no God or religion.

25:27.text: Majesty, viz., of God. For to search into that incomprehensible Majesty, and to pretend to sound the depths of the wisdom of God, is exposing our weak understanding to be blinded with an excess of light and glory, which it cannot comprehend.

When the Church proposes to us any mystery, we have only to believe. Hebrew, "but it is glorious to sound their glory," and see where the wicked end, that we may not envy them, Proverbs 3:31., and Psalm 36:7.
Protestants, "so for men to search their own glory, is not glory," but a sin.

"It is not good to eat too much honey," (Chaldean) or to sound the glorious words of God and wisdom, or the mysteries of religion. Septuagint, "But it is right to reverence glorious speeches," (Calmet) with esteem and humility.

25:28.text: Speaking. He lays himself open to every attack, Proverbs 29:11.

26:1.text: Glory and power. A fool in a high office will endanger himself and the public; (Calmet) while the virtuous, seeing that merit is not regarded, will not push themselves forward.

26:2.text: As a bird, etc. The meaning is, that a curse uttered without cause shall do no harm to the person that is cursed, but will return upon him that curseth; as whithersoever a bird flies, it returns to its own nest.

Come. Chaldean, "shall not come in vain," if it be just, like that of Noe, Josue, etc. Hebrew, "shall not come" (Calmet) to the person against whom it is uttered, though God will not hold the curser guiltless, as the Vulgate intimates. (Haydock) Curses, anathemas, etc., vented without reason, do not injure any but those who denounce them. Yet out of respect for ecclesiastical authority, those who are under censures, must abstain from their functions till they be absolved.

26:3.text: Snaffle. "Bit," or muzzle, (camus) to prevent the animal from biting.
Septuagint, Arabic, etc., "a goad for an ass." But metheg denotes a bridle. (Montanus; Haydock) asses being there very large, and commonly used for riding, Proverbs 13:13.

26:4.text: Answer not a fool, etc. Viz., so as to imitate him; but only so as to reprove his folly.
If thou answer at all, (ver. 5.) do it to the purpose, and to prevent others from taking scandal, (Calmet) as well as to humble the wicked, for his good.

26:6.text: Iniquity. He is exposed to shame, (Septuagint) and must condemn his own folly.

26:7.text: Fair. Hebrew, "unequal legs," or "lifted up," so, etc.

26:8.src: Pole. Syn. Parkhurst in rogom.; Haydock
26:8.text: Mercury. The god of travellers, who were wont to throw a stone at the foot of his statue, as the Indians (Vincent. Bellor. xxiv.) and Arabs did. Mahomet would not disturb this superstitious custom. The Rabbins style these statues Mercolis. But Septuagint, etc., give another sense, "as he that bindeth a stone (Calmet) in the boss of a ring, sphendone, (Menochius) or in a sling," can do no good, but only endanger himself or others, "so," etc. Yet margema is never used elsewhere for a sling, and it means undoubtedly "a heap of stones," (Calmet) as Montanus substitutes instead of "the sling," in Pagnin. "As a small piece of precious stone in a heap of stones is lost, so," etc.
Honour, or an office, in which he may do harm.

26:9.text: If. Hebrew, "as a thorn goeth up into the," etc.
If he attempt to handle or to extract one, he will wound himself the more, as the fool would render truth and wisdom contemptible.
Parable. Septuagint, "but slavery in the hands of fools" groweth up.

26:10.text: Anger, and prevent lawsuits. Hebrew is variously read and translated.

Protestants, "the great God, that formed all things, both rewardeth the fool and rewardeth the transgressors." Marginal note, "a great man giveth all, and he hireth the fool," etc. Septuagint, "all the flesh of fools is exposed to many storms, for their excess is punished."

26:11.text: Dog. This is the only animal which is known to do so. St. Peter uses this comparison to deter any from renouncing the faith; as the Fathers do, to shew the misery attending a relapse. Septuagint here add, "there is a confusion," etc., taken from Ecclesiasticus 4:25.

26:12.text: Fool. The ignorant may be convinced that he wants instruction.
But "none are worse than the half-learned."

26:14.src: St. Augustine, ser. xxii. or clxiv.
26:14.text: Bed, which he will scarcely leave, though avarice push him forward.

26:16.text: Seven, or many wise men, who used to speak in a sententious manner.

26:17.text: Anger. Hebrew, "passeth by, and meddleth with a quarrel not to him" pertaining.
Such expose themselves foolishly to danger. Great discretion is requisite to reconcile those at variance.

26:19.text: Jest. He will not escape, no more than the person who had committed murder involuntarily, if he were not in a city of refuge, Numbers 35:22.

26:20.text: Faileth. Hebrew, Symmachus, Calmet, "aboundeth, the fire is bright, or flourisheth"

26:23.text: Dross. Hence the proud will be detested, and appear contemptible.

26:24.text: Lips. He will speak of what he hates much.

26:26.text: Deceitfully. Hebrew, "in desolation." He will cringe, but when he finds an opportunity, he will discover his evil designs.

26:27.text: Him. "Bad advice is worst to him who gives it."

27:2.text: Lips. All hate affectation and vanity, John 5:51.

27:3.text: Both. He is insupportable to himself and to others, Ecclesiasticus 22:17.

27:4.text: And who. Septuagint, "but envy (zeal) beareth nothing." The more we yield to the envious, the more he is offended at our good behaviour.

27:5.text: Love. Which can be of no service to us, while reproof may cause us to amend.

27:6.text: Enemy. Joab slew Amasa, while he kissed him, 2 Kings 20:9., and Matthew 26:48. True friendship is not attentive to outward appearances.

27:8.text: Place, or vocation, like the prodigal son, Luke xv. The Israelites were much attached to their own country, where they might practise the true religion.

27:9.text: And. Septuagint add, "wine and incense....but accidents tear the soul."

27:10.text: Affliction. He will be less compassionate than a tried friend.
Better, etc. This daily experience evinces. "Those who purchase land, should consider if there be plenty of water, and a neighbour."
The Persians honour most those who live nearest to them.

27:11.text: Thou. Hebrew, Complutensian, and Sixtus V, "I may," etc. Septuagint, "and cast reproaches from thee."

27:14.text: In the night. Or "early in the morning," de nocte, as the Hebrew implies.
Curseth. His importunity will be equally displeasing.
Flattery is dangerous, (Calmet) and unworthy of a free man.

27:16.text: Hand. As it will flow away, such a woman is commonly incorrigible.

27:17.text: Sharpeneth. Or instructeth. Fungar vice cotis.

27:18.text: Glorified. He who serves his master well shall be promoted.

27:19.text: Are. Hebrew, "to men." Our hearts have all something similar.
Septuagint, "as faces are not like each other, so neither are the hearts of men." They have all some peculiarity.
But this agrees not with the original.

27:20.text: Destruction. Hebrew abaddo, or abadon, Proverbs 15:11., and Apocalypse 9:11. People die, and are plunged in hell daily.

27:21.text: Praiseth. If he be not puffed up, or if all agree in his praises, we may conclude that they are well founded.
The, etc., is not in Hebrew, Comp.[Complutensian?], St. Jerome, or Chaldean, and destroys the connection.

27:22.text: Mortar. Such were used by those who could not afford handmills.

27:23.text: Flocks. John 10:3., and Ecclesiasticus 7:24. This may be applied to pastors.

27:24.text: Generation. Thou wilt be cited as an example of prudence, if thou hast forseen the change of thy affairs, and provided for it. In the east it was not unusual to see a general of an army reduced to the meanest condition, and economy is necessary for all.

27:26.text: Field. If thou wishest to purchase, or to pay the workmen.

27:27.text: Milk. We cannot but admire such frugality. Septuagint are rather different; (Calmet) ver. 25., "Be careful of the grass in thy field....that thou mayst have lambs for thy clothing. Honour the field, that there may be lambs for thee. (27) Son, thou hast from me solid instructions for thy life, and for that of thy servants."

28:1.text: Pursueth. "A crime is its own punishment."
Dread. Of any thing terrestrial, as long as the object of his love is not attacked, Romans 8:35.

28:2.text: Princes. Who each contend for the sovereign power.

28:8.text: Poor. It seldom happens that the unjust leave their riches to their children, Proverbs 13:22., and Job 27:16.

28:9.text: His. Septuagint, "he rendereth his prayer abominable."

28:13.text: Mercy. This is true repentance, which enjoins, "not only to bewail past sins, but also to amend."
Sacramental confession was not required of the Jews, but they confessed their sins, when they laid their hands on the victim, etc., Leviticus 4., and 5.

28:16.text: Prudence. Septuagint, "riches." Poverty is a great temptation to people in authority, more than avarice itself.

28:20.text: Innocent. Unpunished, if he employ fraud, ver. 22., and Proverbs 13:21.

28:21.text: Neither small nor great bribes must be taken.

28:22.text: Poverty. Septuagint, "the merciful shall have power over him," ver. 8.

28:28.text: Men. Septuagint, "the just groan," and all are under "apprehensions."

29:8.text: Corrupt. Hebrew, "scoffers," who provoke both God and men.

29:9.text: Rest; or bring him to hear reason.
Septuagint, "a wise man shall rule nations; but the wicked being angry, is laughed at, and does not frighten."

29:10.text: Soul. They wish to protect the upright, Psalm 141:5.

29:13.text: Enlightener. Septuagint, "visitor," with punishment, as they seem to speak of palliated usury, which cannot escape God. See Proverbs 22:2.

29:18.text: Prophecy, by the urim, or by the mouth of prophets, who were in great power and estimation, 3 Kings 1:24., and 12:23., and 2 Paralipomenon 25:6., and 28:8.
Septuagint, "There shall be none to explain the law to the impious nation."
When pastors are wanting, all goes to ruin, 1 Corinthians 11:4.

29:20.text: Amendment. St. Jerome (ad Evag.) says, after Thucydides: "Ignorance produces confidence, and learning fear."

29:24.text: Soul, and exposeth himself to death, though this was only inflicted for stealing a man, Exodus 21:16. The night-thief might also be killed in the fact, Exodus 22:2. But if the person who had stolen denied it on oath, he was put to death for perjury, Leviticus 5:1.

29:25.text: Fall. Human respects will not long preserve him from sin.

Septuagint have a double version: "Those who fear and are ashamed of men, shall be thrown down. But he who confideth in the Lord, shall rejoice. Impiety overturneth man, while he who trusteth in the Lord shall be saved."

30:1.text: Gatherer, etc., or, as it is in the Latin, Congregans, the son of Vomens. The Latin interpreter has given us in this place the signification of the Hebrew names, instead of the names themselves, which are in the Hebrew, Agur, the son of Jakeh. But whether this Agur be the same person as Solomon, as many think, or a different person, whose doctrine was adopted by Solomon, and inserted among his parables or proverbs, is uncertain.

Vomiter may denote David, who delivered many excellent canticles; Eructavit cor, Psalm xliv. De Dieu translates, "The words of him who is recollected the son of obedience." The author styles himself foolish, and asks for neither beggary nor riches, (ver. 2, 8.) which seems not to agree with Solomon; though there can be no doubt but this chapter is inspired.
In effect, that great king might form this petition, being mindful of the instability of human greatness, and confess that of himself he was foolish.
Vision. Hebrew massa (Haydock) generally implies something disagreeable, but here it is put for a collection of moral sentences.

With, etc. Hebrew also, "to Ithiel, even unto Ithiel and Ucal," (Protestants; Haydock) friends of Agur, (Calmet) or his children, (Menochius) or rather Solomon speaks to all the faithful. We never find Agur mentioned as a canonical writer; and if he were, he would have been placed after Solomon.

30:2.text: With me. He speaks of what he could claim of his own, abstracting from the prophetic light.
In his humility, he supposeth that others are more perfect. The wisest know best their own defects.

30:4.text: Descended. How then could I acquire such a sublime science?
Christ alone could impart it, (John 3:13.; Haydock) who is the perfect wisdom.
It is no less difficult to fathom the designs of Providence. Some understand the "spirit" of prophecy to be here meant.
Son. Septuagint, "children." But many Greek copies, and all the interpreters, have Son, which the Fathers explain of the second person of the blessed Trinity, specified [in] Proverbs 8:22.

30:5.text: Is fire-tried; that is, most pure, like gold purified by fire.

30:6.text: And not any thing contrary, Deuteronomy 4:2., and 12:32.
Liar. Our Saviour condemned the false explanations of the Pharisees, as his Church does those of all heretics.

30:8.text: Words, which are so opposite to thine, ver. 5.
Riches. The former often prompts men to deceive, the latter to grow proud and forget God.

30:10.text: Accuse. Septuagint, "deliver not" to an idolater, Deuteronomy 23:15.
Servant. Add not to his affliction.
We must suppose that the accusation is frivolous or false.
A servant may do a person much injury: but this ought not to deter the other from performing what justice and charity require.

30:15.text: The horse-leech: concupiscence, which hath two daughters that are never satisfied, viz., lust and avarice.

30:16.text: Womb. Septuagint, "the love of a woman," (Haydock) a harlot, or rather Hebrew, "a barren woman."

Enough. The more fuel, the brighter the flame. These four similitudes may denote cruelty, lust, avarice, and prodigality; (Calmet) or the first and last may be understood (Haydock) of envy and ambition.

30:17.text: Labour. Septuagint, etc., "old age." Hebrew, "the obedience or admonition." Those who cursed their parents, were sentenced to death, Leviticus 20:6.

30:19.text: Youth. Hebrew, "a virgin." The "conception of a mighty man (the Messias; Haydock) in a virgin," is fitly compared to the flight of an eagle in the air, which leaves no trace behind, and is the most difficult to comprehend. See Jeremias 31:22.; Univ. Hist. 3:p. 144, note. Isaiah 7:14.; Parkhurst in alm.
Some of the Jews have admitted this explanation.

Others understand that the marks of virginity are equivocal; (Bossuet, etc.) or, if we stick to the Vulgate and Septuagint, the difference betwixt a child and a young man is extremely great, and almost incomprehensible.
Young people who follow their carnal appetite, can no more give an account of their actions than of the course of an eagle, etc.
His wanderings are manifold. The Hebrew seems to contain a prophecy of Christ's conception.

30:23.text: Mistress, and is married to her master. She will generally prove insolent; like slaves on the throne, Regnabit sanguine multo ad regnum quisquis venit ab exilio.

30:27.text: Bands, like an army. When one rises or falls, all do the like. They are so numerous in the East, as to darken the sun and spread destruction, Joel i., and 2:

30:28.text: The stellio. A kind of house lizard, marked with spots like stars, from whence it has its name.
It probably provides food against the stormy season, like ants.

30:30.text: Meeteth. If he retreat, he looks back with disdain, till the woods conceal the turpitude of his flight.

30:31.text: Loins. It rules, and is even terrible to lions.
The terms of the original are found nowhere else, and some understand the horse, the bee, and a soldier in arms.

But we know no animal or king of this name; and it may imply, "in the midst of his court," or "assembly." (Chaldean) Some Latin copies read, Et Rex, nec est qui resistat ei, (Sixtus V.) which is more conformable to the Hebrew, (Calmet) and is here translated, though the Vulgate read, Nec est rex qui, etc. These four emblems (Haydock) denote fortitude, chastity, order, and justice.

30:32.text: Mouth. Fools ought not to govern.
Many might have been deemed wise, if they had continued in a lower station.
Hebrew, "If thou hast acted foolishly in raising thyself, and if thou hast entertained evil thoughts, put thy hand to thy mouth."
Chaldean, "put not thy," etc. Give not way to pride, or to insolent language.

30:33.text: And. Hebrew, "For he who presseth milk."
Protestants, "Surely the churning of milk bringeth forth butter," etc.
Strife. Moderation is necessary, (Calmet) in all actions.

31:1.text: Lamuel. This name signifies, God with him; and is supposed to be one of the names of Solomon.
Grotius would explain it of Ezechias. But why should we abandon the tradition of both Jews and Christians?

Mother; Bethsabee, who it seems was inspired, unless she received these maxims from Nathan. Solomon always speaks of her with the utmost respect, as a prudent mother may have the greatest influence over the tender minds of her children, Proverbs 1:8., and 23:25.

31:2.text: Vows. She seems unable to express her concern for him when he first mounted the throne, and shewed her the greatest reverence, 3 Kings 2:19.

31:3.text: Women. This would destroy thy health, and tend to impoverish the kingdom.

31:4.text: Give. Septuagint, "Do all with counsel. By advice drink wine. Lords are inclined to anger; let them not drink wine."

31:5.text: Poor. Solon condemned to death, at Athens, the prince who should get drunk; and the Areopagites excluded from their assembly a judge who had dined in a tavern.

31:6.text: Are sad. Hebrew, "perish," being sentenced to die; (Mark 15:23., and Amos 2:8.) or, who grieve and mourn for one deceased. On such occasions no food was prepared in the house, but the friends supplied what was necessary, and went to eat and drink with the afflicted, Ecclesiastes 7:3.

31:7.text: More. Not that intoxication is permitted even to them.

31:8.text: Pass through life, or the country.
Septuagint, "Open thy mouth and judge righteously. Render justice to the poor and weak."
Doctrine is best received by those who are more ready to hear than to speak.

31:10.text: Who. The following verses are in alphabetical order. They contain a grand eulogy of Bethsabee, who repented, or of a perfect matron.
Such are rare, though they may be found.
Price. Formerly people bought their wives.
Is. Hebrew, "is far above rubies (Protestants; Haydock) or pearls," Lamentations 4:7.

31:11.text: Spoils, taken in war. His wife will supply all necessaries, ver. 21.

31:13.text: Hands, with skill and industry, (Calmet) or "willingness."
Ladies of the highest quality formerly employed themselves in this manner, like Penelope. Alexander, Augustus, and Charlemagne wore garments, which their sisters or wives had wrought.

31:14.text: Bread; all that is used for meat and drink. Septuagint, "riches." Grabe, "livelihood:" Bion.

31:15.text: Night, or early in the morning, as soon as the night was over; de nocte. Hebrew, "while it is yet night."
Extreme vigilance is required of those who direct others. "The master must be first up, and last in bed."

31:16.text: Considered. This conduct is suggested by prudence.
Cato (Rust. 2.) says, "Do not go only once round the field," etc.

31:17.text: Arm; working, and making others obey.

31:18.text: Night, during a great part of which she will work.

31:19.text: Spindle. She purposes and begins well, trusting in God for perfection and a reward.

31:21.text: Domestics. Hebrew, "house is clothed in purple," which may be understood of the domestics, though it seems more probably to refer to her husband and children.

31:22.text: Tapestry, for the beds and floor.

31:23.text: Gates. Chaldean, "provinces." The rich were chosen for judges.

31:24.text: The Chanaanite, the merchant; for Chanaanite, in Hebrew, signifies a merchant.
The Phoenicians travelled into all countries. Traffic was not then deemed a discredit, even to kings.
Girdles were worn both by men and women, and were very costly, insomuch that (Calmet) the kings of Persia assigned cities to furnish their wives with them.
They who practise and teach the law may be said to buy and sell.

31:25.text: Clothing: it is very beautiful, or wisdom and virtue surround her.
Day. She fears not death, (Calmet) or future distress of hunger, etc.

31:26.text: Tongue. She is ever bent on doing good.
Very different from many of her sex, who are taken up with vanity and complaints.

31:27.text: Idle, out of a sense of duty, and not through avarice.

31:28.text: Her. They were best able to judge of her merit.

31:30.text: Lord. Hitherto natural qualifications appear: but to these the Christian matron must add sincere piety: and thus Solomon completes the character of his mother, (Calmet) who had given him such excellent instructions, or of any accomplished woman. Outward beauty soon (Haydock) decays; but the fear of God is more deserving of praise.

31:31.text: Gates, before all the judges (Haydock) and people.
Good works shall be rewarded at God's tribunal, (Worthington) when the vain worldly beauty shall be covered with confusion.
This idea of a perfect woman is best verified in the Catholic Church, (St. Augustine; Ven. Bede) though the blessed Virgin [Mary], etc., may also be designated.
The use of the alphabet herein denotes, that we must begin with a moral good life, if we would penetrate the greater mysteries of the Scriptures.
